heroText: Queenstown is an outdoor travel anchor in New Zealand, built around mountains, lake routes, winter sport and adventure events.

highlights.name: Queenstown
highlights.text: Queenstown is an outdoor travel anchor in New Zealand, built around mountains, lake routes, winter sport and adventure events.

history.label: Origins
history.text: Māori settlement and presence The area was discovered and first settled by Māori.
The level of the lake rose from 310.5 m to 312.77 m, leading to the most severe flooding in Queenstown's recorded history.
The first non-Māori to see Lake Wakatipu was European Nathanael Chalmers who was guided by Reko, the chief of the Tuturau, over the Waimea Plains and up the Mataura River in September 1853.

history.label: 1860s
history.text: The tribe of Kāti Māmoe occupied a settlement called Te Kirikiri Pa where the Queenstown Gardens are today, but by the time European migrants arrived in the 1860s this settlement was no longer being.
In 1860, Rees established a high country farm where Queenstown's town centre now stands, but the discovery of gold in the Arrow River in 1862 encouraged him to convert his wool shed into a.
By the start of 1864 Queenstown had multiple shops, several bars, a post office, a local newspaper, both an Anglican and Catholic church, and a timber yard.

William's Cottage, the Lake Lodge of Ophir (now Artbay Gallery), Queenstown Police Station, and St Peter's Anglican Church lie close together in a designated historic precinct. 1999 flooding There was a severe weather event.

history.label: Growth
history.text: Kāi Tahu say that the lake was dug by the Waitaha ancestor, Rākaihautū, with his kō (digging stick) named Tūwhakaroria.

history.label: Modern city
history.text: After arriving at Whakatū Nelson in the waka Uruao, Rākaihautū divided his crew into two.

history.label: Civic role
history.text: After digging the lakes Hāwea, Wānaka, and Whakatipu Waimāori, he travelled through the Greenstone and Hollyford valleys before finally digging Whakatipu Waitai (Lake McKerrow).

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Evidence of stake nets, baskets for catching eels, spears and ashes indicated the Glenorchy area was visited by Māori.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: The settlement swiftly grew after the discovery of gold with hotels and entertainment venues erected to provide for the prospectors.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Market gardens shortly followed and replaced the need for produce to be transported from Dunedin to the town.

history.label: Regional role
history.text: Properties in Glenorchy and Kingston were also flooded, and the road from Queenstown to Glenorchy was damaged by washouts.

name: Queenstown
